title: Loss Prevention
description: How to identify and safely respond to shoplifting on the sales floor without breaking the law.

steps.title: Signs of potential theft
steps.content: What to watch for: a person wanders 10+ minutes with no apparent purpose, constantly glances at staff or cameras, wears off-season clothing (heavy coat in summer = big pockets), arrives in a group and one distracts you, avoids eye contact, nervously exits and re-enters. No single sign equals guilt — only combinations do.
steps.key_takeaway: Three signs combined = heightened attention. One sign = regular customer.

steps.title: Preventive behavior
steps.content: The most effective move is to signal your presence. Approach, greet: 'Hello, can I help you find something?'. 80% of thieves leave after this step. Walk the floor, don't stand behind the register all day. Eye contact says 'I see you'.
steps.key_takeaway: Greeting = 80% prevention. Thieves don't steal where they've been noticed.

steps.title: Observe, don't pursue
steps.content: If suspicion strengthens — observe from a distance, don't trail them. Radio security: 'Attention, sales floor, aisle X, male in blue jacket'. Call a colleague, don't be alone. Don't film on your phone without consent — that violates privacy laws.
steps.key_takeaway: Observe, don't pursue. Unfounded pursuit = defamation lawsuit.

steps.title: What NEVER to do
steps.content: Prohibited: physically restrain before they cross the register line (illegal until theft is completed), search the customer (police-only authority), threaten, yell, insult, publicly accuse without proof, grab clothing, block the exit. Violation = false imprisonment charge.
steps.key_takeaway: Before register — you observe. After register without payment — you call police. Never touch.

steps.title: The moment of crossing the register
steps.content: Theft is legally complete after crossing the register line without payment. Calmly approach: 'Hi there, did you maybe forget to pay for an item?'. Gives them a chance to explain — half the cases resolve without police. Lead them to a separate area.
steps.key_takeaway: First question is not 'why did you steal', but 'maybe you forgot to pay'. This matters legally.

steps.title: Calling police and documentation
steps.content: If theft is confirmed: call police (911/112), security guard detains (only licensed security — not you). Draft a detention report: date, time, circumstances, witnesses, item, value. Preserve camera footage until police arrive. Original to police, copy to store archive.
steps.key_takeaway: Licensed security detains. You are a witness and documentarian.

steps.title: Analysis and prevention
steps.content: After an incident: team debrief at morning huddle (no customer names, only patterns), review blind spots, relocate high-risk items closer to the register, add a camera. Repeated theft of the same item = likely an insider, not an outsider.
steps.key_takeaway: Repeated theft from a locked stockroom = look at staff, not the street.

quiz.question: How many theft signs must align for suspicion?
quiz.explanation: One sign = regular customer. Three or more = heightened attention.

quiz.question: Most effective prevention?
options: Greeting and contact — 80% of thieves leave
quiz.explanation: A greeting signals presence. Thieves avoid places where they've been seen.

quiz.question: Can you physically detain a customer on the floor?
options: No — theft isn't complete until register line is crossed
quiz.explanation: Before the register, theft isn't complete — detention = false imprisonment.

quiz.question: First phrase to a suspect after the register?
options: 'Did you maybe forget to pay for an item?'
quiz.explanation: Polite framing gives a chance to explain — resolves 50% of cases without police.

quiz.question: Repeat theft of the same item from stockroom — who to look at?
quiz.explanation: Repeat theft from a locked zone = insider. Retail stat: 40% of loss is staff.

manager_checklist: Staff knows the 6 signs of potential theft
Walks the floor, doesn't stay behind the register all day
Uses the phrasing 'did you maybe forget to pay'
Documents incidents with a report and preserves footage
Reviews theft patterns at team huddles

kpi.how_to_measure: Incident log: observations vs completed thefts
